<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  table.plain td.num{font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;color:#cbd5e1}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-begin -->
<script src="./js/display-prefs.js"></script>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-end -->
</head>
rawBodyPrefix:
← Notebook
1 The question 2 Fermat's two squares 3 Gaussian integers 4 Two squares in general 5 Three squares 6 Four squares 7 Waring 8 Forward: theta

Sums of squares

Which integers are sums of two, three, or four squares? The answers run through Fermat's descent, the Gaussian integers, Legendre–Gauss, Lagrange, and finally the generating function $\theta(\tau)^k$ that connects it all to modular forms.

blocks.html:
1. The question

For $k\ge 1$ and $n\ge 0$, let $$r_k(n)=\#\bigl\{(x_1,\ldots,x_k)\in\mathbb{Z}^k : x_1^2+\cdots+x_k^2=n\bigr\},$$ the number of ways to write $n$ as an ordered sum of $k$ integer squares (with signs and zero allowed). The natural questions split in two:

  • Qualitative. For which $n$ is $r_k(n)>0$?
  • Quantitative. Can we compute $r_k(n)$ in closed form?

Here is the raw data for small $n$ and $k$:

params.title: $r_k(n)$ for small $n,\,k$

layout.html: Row $n=0$ gives $r_k(0)=1$ (all zeros). Note the gaps in $r_2$ (no reps for $3,6,7,11,12,\ldots$), the single gap $r_3(7)=0$, and that $r_4(n)\ge 1$ for every $n$ — foreshadowing Lagrange.

blocks.html: The goal is to understand why these patterns appear, first by elementary number theory (Fermat, Gauss, Lagrange) and then by recognising the $r_k(n)$ as Fourier coefficients of a modular form.

params.title: $r_k(n)$ for small $n,\,k$

layout.html: Row $n=0$ gives $r_k(0)=1$ (all zeros). Note the gaps in $r_2$ (no reps for $3,6,7,11,12,\ldots$), the single gap $r_3(7)=0$, and that $r_4(n)\ge 1$ for every $n$ — foreshadowing Lagrange.

blocks.html:
2. Fermat's two-squares theorem

The key prime-level result, conjectured by Girard (1625), claimed by Fermat (1640), and proved by Euler by infinite descent (1749):

Theorem (Fermat). An odd prime $p$ is a sum of two squares, $$p=a^2+b^2,\qquad a,b\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0},$$ if and only if $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$. The representation is then unique up to order and signs. (The prime $p=2=1^2+1^2$ is the exceptional case.)

The easy direction

If $p=a^2+b^2$ with $p$ odd, reduce mod $4$: squares are $0$ or $1$, so $a^2+b^2\in\{0,1,2\}\pmod 4$. Since $p$ is odd, we need $a^2+b^2\equiv 1\pmod 4$, hence $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$.

The hard direction: Euler's descent

Assume $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$. Then $-1$ is a quadratic residue mod $p$ (by Euler's criterion, $(-1)^{(p-1)/2}=1$), so there is $m$ with $m^2\equiv -1\pmod p$, giving $m^2+1^2\equiv 0\pmod p$. Thus $p\mid a^2+b^2$ for some $a,b$ not both divisible by $p$. Now the descent: starting from a multiple $kp=a^2+b^2$ with $k

The identity says: the set of integers that are sums of two squares is closed under multiplication. This is a shadow of multiplicativity in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ — the true home of the theorem.

params.title: Brahmagupta–Fibonacci multiplier
params.hint: multiply two sums of two squares

params.title: Brahmagupta–Fibonacci multiplier
params.hint: multiply two sums of two squares

blocks.html:
3. Why it works: the Gaussian integers

Fermat's theorem is the surface of a deeper structural fact. Pass from $\mathbb{Z}$ to the Gaussian integers $$\mathbb{Z}[i]=\{a+bi : a,b\in\mathbb{Z}\}\subset\mathbb{C}.$$ The norm $N(a+bi)=a^2+b^2$ is multiplicative, and $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ is a Euclidean domain (division with remainder in $\mathbb{C}$, then round to nearest Gaussian integer), hence a unique factorization domain. Writing $p=a^2+b^2$ means exactly $p=(a+bi)(a-bi)$, i.e. the rational prime $p$ factors in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$.

Whether a rational prime $p$ factors in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ is controlled by the splitting behaviour of the ideal $(p)$:

$p$behaviour in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$factorisationcondition
$2$ramified$(2)=(1+i)^2\cdot(\text{unit})$$p=2$
$p\equiv 1\pmod 4$split$(p)=\mathfrak{p}\bar{\mathfrak{p}}$$(-1/p)=+1$
$p\equiv 3\pmod 4$inert$(p)$ stays prime$(-1/p)=-1$

The splitting is controlled by the Legendre symbol $(-1/p)$, which is $+1$ iff $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ by Euler's criterion — a special case of the broader machinery developed on the quadratic reciprocity page. The theorem of Fermat is then a one-line corollary:

$p$ splits in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ iff $p=\pi\bar\pi=N(\pi)=a^2+b^2$. And $p$ splits iff $(-1/p)=+1$ iff $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$.

Visually: the Gaussian integers form the square lattice in $\mathbb{C}$. The primes $\pi\in\mathbb{Z}[i]$ above a rational prime $p$ are exactly the lattice points on the circle $|\alpha|^2=p$. For $p=5$ there are eight such points ($(\pm 1\pm 2i)$ and $(\pm 2\pm i)$, giving four associate pairs — one prime up to units); for $p=3$ the circle $|\alpha|^2=3$ contains no lattice points at all.

params.title: Gaussian integer lattice

blocks.html:
Used in
Sums of squares · Legendre three-square theorem

params.title: Gaussian integer lattice

blocks.html:
4. Two squares for general $n$

Multiplicativity in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ lets us read off the answer for composite $n$. Factor $n=2^{e_0}\prod_{p\equiv 1} p^{e_p}\prod_{q\equiv 3} q^{f_q}$.

Theorem. $n$ is a sum of two squares iff every prime $q\equiv 3\pmod 4$ appears in $n$ to an even power — i.e. every $f_q$ is even.

Reason: each $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ splits as $\pi\bar\pi$, so $p^{e_p}=(\pi\bar\pi)^{e_p}$ is freely a norm. Each $q\equiv 3\pmod 4$ is inert; $q^{f_q}$ has norm $q^{2f_q}$ inside $\mathbb{Z}[i]$, and to be a norm of a single Gaussian integer it must come in pairs, forcing $f_q$ even. The prime $2$ is ramified and never an obstruction.

Jacobi's counting formula

More precisely, counting ordered signed representations:

Theorem (Jacobi). For $n\ge 1$, $$r_2(n)=4\bigl(d_1(n)-d_3(n)\bigr),$$ where $d_j(n)$ is the number of positive divisors of $n$ congruent to $j\pmod 4$.

E.g. $n=25$: divisors $1,5,25$, all $\equiv 1\pmod 4$, so $r_2(25)=4(3-0)=12$. And indeed $25=0^2+(\pm 5)^2=(\pm 3)^2+(\pm 4)^2$, which with ordering gives $4+8=12$. The factor of $4$ is the unit group $\{\pm 1,\pm i\}$ of $\mathbb{Z}[i]$.

params.title: Two-squares checker

blocks.html:
See also
  • Quadratic reciprocity law

Used in
Sums of squares · Gaussian integers and prime splitting

params.title: Two-squares checker

blocks.html:
5. Three squares: Legendre–Gauss

Sums of three squares are more delicate — they do not form a semigroup under multiplication. Legendre (1798) and Gauss (1801) settled the question:

Theorem (Legendre–Gauss). A non-negative integer $n$ is a sum of three squares iff $n$ is not of the form $4^a(8b+7)$ for some $a,b\ge 0$.

The easy direction: if $n=x^2+y^2+z^2$, reduce mod $8$. Squares mod $8$ are $\{0,1,4\}$, so sums of three squares mod $8$ miss the value $7$; a quick 2-adic argument then rules out $4^a(8b+7)$. The hard direction requires the theory of ternary quadratic forms and genus theory; Gauss showed that $r_3(n)$ is essentially a class number of imaginary quadratic orders — a remarkable bridge to algebraic number theory.

Gauss's exact formula: for $n$ squarefree, $n>3$, $$r_3(n)=\begin{cases} 12\,h(-4n), & n\equiv 1,2\pmod 4,\\ 24\,h(-n), & n\equiv 3\pmod 8,\\ 0, & n\equiv 7\pmod 8,\end{cases}$$ where $h(D)$ is the class number of discriminant $D$. This is the first appearance of a class number in the count of something concrete.

params.title: Three-squares: excluded integers
params.hint: red = $4^a(8b+7)$, not a sum of three squares

blocks.html:
See also
  • Genus theory

Used in
Sums of squares · Four squares and Waring perspective

params.title: Three-squares: excluded integers
params.hint: red = $4^a(8b+7)$, not a sum of three squares

blocks.html:
6. Four squares: Lagrange

Finally, everything becomes a sum of four squares:

Theorem (Lagrange, 1770). Every non-negative integer $n$ is a sum of four integer squares: $$n=a^2+b^2+c^2+d^2.$$

The standard modern proof mirrors Fermat's descent:

  1. Multiplicativity. Sums of four squares are closed under multiplication, via Euler's four-square identity $$(a_1^2+a_2^2+a_3^2+a_4^2)(b_1^2+b_2^2+b_3^2+b_4^2) = c_1^2+c_2^2+c_3^2+c_4^2$$ for explicit $c_i$ that are bilinear in $a,b$. (This is the norm form of the Hurwitz quaternions $\mathbb{H}_\mathbb{Z}$, exactly as Brahmagupta–Fibonacci is the norm form of $\mathbb{Z}[i]$.)
  2. Reduce to primes. By (1), it suffices to show every prime $p$ is a sum of four squares.
  3. Prime step. For odd prime $p$, the sets $\{a^2\bmod p\}$ and $\{-1-b^2\bmod p\}$ each have $(p+1)/2$ elements, so they intersect: $\exists a,b$ with $a^2+b^2\equiv -1\pmod p$, i.e. $mp=a^2+b^2+1$ for some $m
  4. Descent. Starting from a positive multiple $mp$ with $1\le m

Jacobi improved Lagrange to a count:

Theorem (Jacobi). $r_4(n)=8\sum_{d\mid n,\,4\nmid d} d$. In particular for odd $n$, $r_4(n)=8\sigma(n)$.

The formula arises from comparing two expressions for $\theta(\tau)^4$: the generating-function expansion $\sum r_4(n)q^n$ on one side, and the $q$-expansion of an Eisenstein series of weight $2$ (on $\Gamma_0(4)$) on the other.

params.title: Four-squares decomposer

blocks.html:
Used in
Sums of squares · Waring's problem: g(k) and G(k)
Waring's problem and the circle method · Waring's problem: g(k)

params.title: Four-squares decomposer

blocks.html:
7. Waring's problem

If four squares suffice for every $n$, what about higher powers? Waring asked (1770): is there, for each $k\ge 1$, a finite number $g(k)$ such that every positive integer is a sum of at most $g(k)$ $k$-th powers?

Theorem (Hilbert, 1909). $g(k)$ is finite for every $k\ge 1$.

Sharper variants:

  • $g(k)$ — the smallest $s$ such that every $n\ge 1$ is a sum of $s$ $k$-th powers.
  • $G(k)$ — the smallest $s$ such that every sufficiently large $n$ is a sum of $s$ $k$-th powers. $G(k)\le g(k)$, with strict inequality the interesting case.

Small known values:

params.title: Waring constants
params.hint: click a row for a decomposition of a random $n$

params.title: Waring constants
params.hint: click a row for a decomposition of a random $n$

blocks.html:
8. Forward: $\theta$-series and modular forms

All of these counts $r_k(n)$ are Fourier coefficients of a single object. Define the Jacobi theta series $$\theta(\tau)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}} q^{n^2},\qquad q=e^{2\pi i\tau},\ \tau\in\mathbb{H}.$$ Squaring the series and counting coefficients by total exponent, $$\theta(\tau)^k = \sum_{n\ge 0} r_k(n)\,q^n.$$ So every question about $r_k(n)$ is a question about the $q$-expansion of $\theta^k$.

params.title: $r_2(n)$ for $n=1..100$
params.hint: hover a bar to see a decomposition

blocks.html:
The key miracle, due to Jacobi, is that $\theta(\tau)$ is a modular form of weight $1/2$ on a congruence subgroup — more precisely, $\theta(\tau)$ satisfies functional equations under $\tau\mapsto \tau+1$ (trivially) and $\tau\mapsto -1/4\tau$ (via Poisson summation). Hence $\theta^k$ is a modular form of weight $k/2$, and the ring of modular forms on $\Gamma_0(4)$ is finite-dimensional in each weight. This forces identities like $\theta^4=1+8\sum_{d}\sigma^{(2)}(d) q^d$ (an Eisenstein series), recovering Jacobi's $r_4(n)=8\sum_{d\mid n,\,4\nmid d}d$ for free.

The same machinery powers:

  • $r_6(n)$, $r_8(n)$, $\ldots$ — closed forms from Eisenstein series of higher weight;
  • the deep formula for $r_3(n)$ in terms of class numbers (a weight-$3/2$ modular form);
  • and, transposing to elliptic curves, the modularity theorem for $E/\mathbb{Q}$.

Two pages in this notebook will develop the tools:

  • theta-functions.html — $\theta$ as an entire object: transformation laws, Jacobi's triple product, applications to partitions.
  • modular-forms.html — modular forms for $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ and congruence subgroups, Eisenstein and cusp forms, Hecke operators, and the identification of $\theta^k$.

So: the puzzle of which integers are sums of $k$ squares, which took two hundred years to untangle by elementary means, turns out to be one equation among generating functions on the upper half-plane — a story continued at upper-half-plane-hyperbolic.html and onward.

params.title: $r_2(n)$ for $n=1..100$
